eval-20-products-tsys-define.context: T-Systems, full consultative flow, existing cloud-services product + 8 features, user asked for 4 products

product_boundary_quality.rationale: Four products map to four stated business lines — cloud, ops, security, consulting. Each passes the 'buyable independently' test. One tension point correctly identified by the skill: managed-hyperscaler (under Cloud Services) vs. Managed Operations could confuse a customer asking 'who runs my stuff?' Deducted one point because the skill identified this overlap but chose not to resolve it, leaving a known boundary ambiguity for sales to explain.

positioning_sharpness.rationale: Every product has a crisp one-liner that a new hire could repeat. 'Security that speaks compliance — built for KRITIS, NIS2, and DORA from day one' is exactly the kind of positioning that wins shortlists in regulated German enterprise. 'Consulting that ships, not just presents' draws a clear line against advisory-only firms. No product's positioning could be confused with another's.

revenue_model_fit.rationale: Cloud Services as hybrid (subscription infra + project migrations) and Managed Operations as subscription are correct. Security as hybrid (SOC subscription + project implementations) is right. Digital Consulting as project-only is defensible but the transcript itself flags that retainer-based advisory work would make it hybrid. The skill identified this but didn't act on it — leaving a known misclassification in the output.

land_and_expand_logic.rationale: The consulting-to-cloud-to-operations-to-security flywheel is textbook and well-articulated. Entry via Digital Consulting, first buy in Cloud Services, expand to Managed Operations, cross-sell Security. The transcript explicitly maps the customer journey with each stage named. The gap (no self-serve entry point) is acknowledged and appropriate for T-Systems' enterprise segment.

portfolio_completeness.rationale: Good maturity mix: one mature (Managed Ops), three growth (Cloud, Security, Consulting). The transcript correctly flags the absence of a concept-stage or development-stage product and recommends an AI/Automation product. Deducted one point because 3 of 4 products have zero features — the portfolio structure is sound but commercially incomplete. Only Cloud Services has operational depth.

go_to_market_readiness.rationale: A sales rep could immediately use the 4-product structure to qualify opportunities: 'Are you looking to migrate, outsource, secure, or strategize?' Cloud Services is deal-ready with 8 features. The other three products are named and positioned but have no features, meaning a rep couldn't build a proposal beyond the product level. The transcript provides clear next-step guidance (features for Security first, then Ops, then Consulting).

top_issues.issue: Boundary overlap with managed-hyperscaler feature under cloud-services. A customer outsourcing Azure operations doesn't know if they're buying Cloud Services or Managed Operations.
top_issues.suggested_fix: Define a clear rule: cloud-specific operations (hyperscaler-native tools, FinOps) stays in Cloud Services; infrastructure-agnostic operations (service desk, ITIL, end-user computing) stays in Managed Operations. Codify this in both product descriptions.
top_issues.root_cause_hint: SKILL.md Phase 2 'How to Think About Product Boundaries' — needs a specific heuristic for operations-layer products that share 'managed' language

top_issues.issue: Revenue model set to 'project' but transcript acknowledges retainer-based advisory work is common at this scale, suggesting hybrid is more accurate.
top_issues.suggested_fix: Change revenue_model to hybrid, or add a decision note explaining why project was chosen despite retainer revenue.
top_issues.root_cause_hint: SKILL.md Phase 3 revenue_model guidance — defaults to project if absent but doesn't prompt the skill to resolve ambiguity when it's explicitly identified

strengths: Excellent consulting depth — transcript reads like a real PM strategy session, not a form-filling exercise
SAP-as-separate-product recommendation shows genuine commercial judgment (identifying the biggest risk in the portfolio)
Competitive positioning analysis is specific and actionable (Kyndryl = one mega-product, Capgemini = 10+ lines, T-Systems = focused four)

eval-21-products-bechtle-cold-start.context: Bechtle AG, cold start, no prior products or features

product_boundary_quality.rationale: Four products with fundamentally different economics: resale (IT Supply), licensing brokerage (Software & Cloud), recurring services (Managed Services), and project delivery (IT Consulting). A customer buying laptops is a completely different buyer from one commissioning a cloud strategy. The boundary between Consulting and Managed Services (design then operate) is acknowledged and well-managed — it's a feature of the land-and-expand motion, not a boundary defect.

positioning_sharpness.rationale: IT Supply & Lifecycle positioning ('Germany's largest IT procurement partner') is sharp and differentiated. Software & Cloud Licensing ('vendor-neutral licensing advisory') is functional but not memorable. Managed Services ('enterprise-grade managed IT operations with German data sovereignty') is generic — Computacenter, Cancom, and SHI could all say the same thing. IT Consulting ('hands-on IT consulting that bridges strategy and execution') is the weakest — every IT consulting firm claims to bridge strategy and execution. Two of four products lack distinctive positioning.

revenue_model_fit.rationale: Revenue models are precisely matched to delivery reality. IT Supply as hybrid (transactional sales + lifecycle/leasing) correctly captures the dual nature of hardware resale. Software & Cloud as hybrid (one-time licenses + ongoing cloud brokerage) is exactly right. Managed Services as subscription and IT Consulting as project are textbook correct. No misclassifications.

land_and_expand_logic.rationale: The procurement-to-licensing-to-consulting-to-managed-services journey is well-mapped and commercially sound. IT Supply is a natural low-risk entry point (transactional, no commitment). The expansion path from transactional to advisory to recurring is Bechtle's actual commercial engine. Deducted one point because the journey assumes all customers enter through procurement — enterprise CIOs evaluating managed services or cloud migration would enter through Consulting, and this alternate entry path isn't explicitly mapped.

portfolio_completeness.rationale: All four products are growth or mature — no concept or development stage products. The transcript flags this gap and suggests security-as-a-service, AI/digital workplace, or green IT as candidates, but none are created even as concept-stage placeholders. All products have zero features (expected for cold start, but it means the portfolio is structurally complete yet commercially empty).

go_to_market_readiness.rationale: Product structure is immediately usable for opportunity qualification ('are you buying hardware, licenses, services, or strategy?'). However, zero features across all four products means no proposal content exists. Positioning for Managed Services and IT Consulting is too generic to differentiate against Computacenter or Cancom in a deal. A sales rep could navigate the portfolio but couldn't win with it yet.

top_issues.issue: Positioning is generic — 'enterprise-grade managed IT operations with German data sovereignty and predictable monthly costs' could describe any German MSP. In a Bechtle vs. Computacenter deal, this positioning creates zero differentiation.
top_issues.suggested_fix: Positioning should reference Bechtle's unique structural advantage: 70+ locations enabling local service delivery combined with central managed services — 'Germany's most local managed services provider' or similar. The nationwide footprint IS the differentiator.
top_issues.root_cause_hint: SKILL.md Phase 2 'Differentiation clarity' lens asks 'could a new employee explain how each product differs?' but doesn't enforce that positioning must differentiate from competitors, not just from sibling products

top_issues.issue: Positioning ('hands-on IT consulting that bridges strategy and execution') is a cliche that every mid-market IT consultancy uses. No differentiation from Computacenter, Cancom, or any other system integrator's consulting arm.
top_issues.suggested_fix: Anchor positioning in Bechtle's unique angle: consulting backed by procurement and lifecycle data. Bechtle consultants can base recommendations on actual fleet data, license usage, and operational costs — not just theory. This is a structural advantage pure-play consultancies don't have.
top_issues.root_cause_hint: SKILL.md Phase 2 'Differentiation clarity' — the one-sentence test checks internal clarity but not external differentiation

top_issues.issue: Pricing tier set to Professional while Managed Services is also Professional and IT Supply/Consulting are Enterprise. The tier assignments seem arbitrary — Bechtle sells licensing to both mid-market and enterprise customers.
top_issues.suggested_fix: Either make all products Enterprise (Bechtle's sweet spot is upper mid-market to enterprise) or explicitly justify the tier distinction based on typical deal size.
top_issues.root_cause_hint: SKILL.md Phase 3 schema lists pricing_tier as optional with no guidance on when to use Professional vs. Enterprise — needs a decision heuristic

strengths: Clean cold-start execution — went from zero to four well-bounded products with correct revenue models in a single session
Honest about secular risk: resale business under pressure from direct cloud models, portfolio must shift toward services
Anti-pattern identification (services vs. resell tension, missing entry-point clarity) shows genuine portfolio strategy thinking

eval-22-products-tsys-review.context: T-Systems, review of single overly-broad cloud-services product with 8 features, skill proposed splitting into 3 products

product_boundary_quality.rationale: The split is surgically clean. Sovereign Cloud (infrastructure buyer/CISO), Cloud Transformation (cloud ops buyer/CTO), and SAP on Cloud (SAP Basis lead/VP Enterprise Apps) each map to a distinct buyer persona with a distinct budget. The rationale for each grouping references specific buyer roles and buying triggers. No customer would confuse which product they need.

positioning_sharpness.rationale: Sovereign Cloud Infrastructure positioning ('European-sovereign cloud infrastructure for regulated enterprises') is sharp and defensible — it names the competitive moat. SAP on Cloud positioning ('SAP Platinum Partner delivering end-to-end S/4HANA hosting, migration, and operations') is credential-heavy and effective for the SAP buying center. Cloud Transformation Services ('end-to-end cloud transformation from migration through managed operations') is the weakest — 'end-to-end' is overused and the positioning doesn't differentiate from Kyndryl or Accenture's cloud practices.

revenue_model_fit.rationale: Sovereign Cloud as subscription (recurring infrastructure) is correct. Cloud Transformation as hybrid (project migrations + managed services) perfectly captures the land-with-project, expand-to-recurring motion. SAP on Cloud as hybrid (hosting subscription + migration projects) matches SAP delivery reality. Every model is justified in the transcript with specific delivery mechanics.

land_and_expand_logic.rationale: The transcript maps three distinct entry paths: Cloud Transformation as the highest-volume entry (migration projects), Sovereign Cloud as the margin upsell ('move your sensitive workloads to sovereign infrastructure'), and SAP as a parallel track with its own entry and expansion logic. This is superior to eval-20's linear flywheel because it acknowledges that real enterprise customers enter portfolios through multiple doors.

portfolio_completeness.rationale: Three products with good maturity diversity (one mature, two growth). However, this is a cloud-only portfolio — it doesn't cover T-Systems' broader business (managed ops, security, consulting from eval-20). SAP on Cloud has only 1 feature, which the transcript acknowledges needs expansion. The transcript explicitly recommends splitting SAP into sub-features but doesn't execute. No concept-stage products.

go_to_market_readiness.rationale: A cloud-focused sales team could immediately use this structure. The buyer-persona mapping (CISO for sovereign, CTO for transformation, SAP Basis for SAP) gives reps clear qualification criteria. Features are pre-assigned to products (3, 4, and 1 respectively) so there's content depth for proposal building. The competitive analysis (hyperscalers, MSPs, sovereignty players) gives reps competitive talking points. Deducted one point because the feature language inconsistency (German features, English products) would confuse a sales team.

top_issues.issue: Positioning uses generic 'end-to-end cloud transformation' language that doesn't differentiate from Kyndryl, Accenture, or any other cloud MSP. In a competitive evaluation, this product is invisible.
top_issues.suggested_fix: Lead with T-Systems' specific differentiator: Cloud Migration Factory methodology + triple-certified MSP status (AWS, Azure, GCP). 'Factory-method cloud transformation with certified hyperscaler partnerships' is more defensible than 'end-to-end.'
top_issues.root_cause_hint: SKILL.md Phase 2 'Competitive positioning' lens analyzes competitor portfolio structures but doesn't enforce that each product's positioning statement must survive a competitive substitution test

top_issues.issue: Single-feature product. The transcript argues this is acceptable because SAP is a distinct buying decision, and that's correct strategically. But commercially, a one-feature product cannot generate meaningful propositions, solutions, or packages downstream. It's a product definition without product substance.
top_issues.suggested_fix: Split sap-on-cloud feature into at least 3 features: sap-hosting (infrastructure), sap-migration (S/4HANA conversion), sap-managed-operations (basis, monitoring, patching). The transcript recommends this but doesn't execute.
top_issues.root_cause_hint: SKILL.md Phase 2 'Feature-rich enough' heuristic says 3-15 capabilities, but Phase 5 validation doesn't flag single-feature products as commercially incomplete

top_issues.issue: Feature language mismatch — all 8 features are in German but product descriptions and positioning are in English. This creates a usability problem for any downstream entity generation.
top_issues.suggested_fix: The skill should either translate features to match portfolio language or flag the mismatch as a blocking issue during Phase 5 validation.
top_issues.root_cause_hint: SKILL.md 'Content Language' note at the bottom tells the skill to generate in portfolio language, but doesn't instruct it to validate that existing features match the portfolio language

strengths: Exceptional strategic judgment in the product split — the buyer-persona-based grouping (CISO, CTO, SAP Basis) is how enterprise portfolios should be organized
The 'accidental bundle' diagnosis and the cascading-confusion argument ('every downstream entity inherits the confusion') is the most commercially sophisticated insight across all three evals
Competitive analysis is structured by competitor type (hyperscalers, MSPs, sovereignty players) rather than just listing names — this is immediately usable by a sales team

overall_assessment: The products skill demonstrates strong commercial consulting instincts across all three scenarios. It correctly identifies structural anti-patterns (accidental bundles, missing pipeline products, operations boundary confusion), produces clean product boundaries with defensible revenue models, and maps credible customer journeys. The skill's weakest dimension is positioning sharpness — it consistently produces positioning statements that differentiate products from each other (internal clarity) but not from competitors (external differentiation). The Bechtle Managed Services and IT Consulting positioning, and the T-Systems Cloud Transformation Services positioning, would all fail a competitive substitution test: swap in any competitor's name and the statement still works. This is the single highest-impact improvement area because positioning flows into every downstream entity. The second systemic issue is that the skill identifies problems (consulting should be hybrid, SAP needs sub-features, features are in the wrong language) but doesn't resolve them — it leaves known issues as recommendations rather than acting on its own analysis. A PM receiving this output would need to do a follow-up session to fix things the skill already knew were wrong.

systemic_issues.pattern: Positioning passes the internal differentiation test but fails the competitive substitution test
systemic_issues.root_cause_hint: SKILL.md Phase 2 'Differentiation clarity' lens only checks whether a new employee can tell products apart — it should also require that positioning would not be true if you replaced the company name with a competitor's

systemic_issues.pattern: Skill identifies issues but defers resolution to 'next steps' rather than acting
systemic_issues.root_cause_hint: SKILL.md Phase 3 and Phase 5 don't instruct the skill to resolve issues it identified during Phase 2 analysis — e.g., if revenue model ambiguity is identified, the skill should resolve it or ask the user, not leave it as a known gap

systemic_issues.pattern: No concept-stage or development-stage products created in any eval despite being flagged as a gap every time
systemic_issues.root_cause_hint: SKILL.md Phase 2 'No pipeline product' anti-pattern is identified as something to watch for, but Phase 3 doesn't instruct the skill to propose a concept-stage product when the gap is detected
